entry.title: Pre Hook External Hook calling V2 REST API returns "Connection reset by peer" - SAP Sales Cloud V2
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am implementing a duplicate CNPJ validation using an External Pre Hook
on the Account entity in SAP Sales Cloud V2. The hook is built in Java
(Spring Boot) deployed on BTP Cloud Foundry.

SCENARIO:
When a user saves an Account with an identification of type Z0001 (CNPJ),
my Pre Hook fires and should check if any other account already has the
same CNPJ. If a duplicate is found, the save should be blocked.

PROBLEM:
When the Pre Hook fires and my service tries to call V2's REST API to
check for duplicates, V2 resets the connection:

ERROR: recvAddress(..) failed: Connection reset by peer
Request to GET https://<tenant>.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/
account-service/accounts?filter=identifications/identificationId
eq 'X'&top=2

Because the connection is reset, my catch block fires and returns
noChanges:true (fail-open), allowing the duplicate CNPJ to be saved.

HOOK CONFIGURATION:
Object: Account Service → Account
Event: Pre Hook
Method: POST
Auth: Basic Auth (Communication System)

MY QUESTION:
Is it expected that V2's REST API cannot be called from within a
Pre Hook on the same entity? Is the transaction lock preventing
inbound API calls during hook execution?

I found this blog post that uses a BTP Destination with CAP + OAuth
to call V2 from within a hook:
https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/
validation-using-external-hooks-in-sap-sales-cloud-v2/ba-p/14120109

Is using a BTP Destination with OAuth (instead of Basic Auth) the
correct way to call V2 from within a Pre Hook? Or is there another
recommended approach?

WHAT I ALREADY TRIED:
1. Direct WebClient call with Basic Auth → Connection reset by peer
2. Increasing timeout to 8 seconds → same result
3. The exact same API call works perfectly OUTSIDE the hook context

RESPONSE FORMAT:
My service correctly returns the following when a duplicate is found:
{
"noChanges": true,
"error": [{
"code": "DUPLICATE_CNPJ",
"message": "CNPJ already registered on account: X",
"target": "identifications"
}]
}
Content-Type: application/json;charset=UTF-8

But because the V2 API call fails first, the catch block returns
noChanges:true before the error response is ever built.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Working with DMS in SAP Build Process Automation — A Complete API Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: In one of my recent projects, I had a scenario where I needed to handle Excel files to create or modify mass cost centers. For this, I definitely needed a way to manage files — so I used DMS (Document Management Service)to handle the file operations. 

DMS (Document Management Service) is a key service in SAP BTP for managing files in the cloud. When working with SAP Build Process Automation (BPA), DMS serves as the file storage — when a user uploads a file through a Form Trigger, it is stored in DMS.

Note: Upload attachments to forms, you need an active SAP Document Management Service subscription. With an active subscription, you must then configure your attachment destinations. Before an attachment destination is configured, the attachment option isn't visible when editing a form (Configure SAP Document Management Service for Process Attachments | SAP Help Portal).

More importantly, you need to know how to download files from DMS. Consider a scenario where you need to validate an uploaded file — say, perform Excel-based validations while the file is on the local desktop. After all processing is complete, you can delete the file from DMS. This is the right approach for maintaining storage hygiene in certain scenarios (not all).

You should also understand how to upload files to DMS. Before moving into BPA development, it is essential to test the DMS APIs independently using Bruno (or any other API testing tool like Postman). This blog walks you through exactly that.

Prerequisites

  1. SAP BTP Subaccount with DMS entitlement enabled
  2. DMS Service Instance & Service Key created
  3. Bruno (or any API testing tool) installed
  4. DMS Destination configured in SAP BTP
    • To create a destination from scratch, refer to the official documentation:
      Create Destinations from Scratch – SAP Help
    • After creating the destination, it will appear under Control Tower → Destinations
    • Use a testing tool to verify the connection is working before integrating with BPA

Note: Before connecting BPA directly to the destination, always validate the APIs independently using Bruno.

Step 1: Authentication – Get Access Token 

To perform any CRUD operation on DMS, you need a Bearer Token for authentication. Here's how to obtain it in Bruno:

  1. Open the Auth tab
  2. Select OAuth 2.0 from the dropdown
  3. Set Grant Type to Client Credentials
  4. Fill in the following fields:

Name Value

Access Token URL

<token-url>/oauth/token

Client ID

<your-client-id>

Client Secret

<your-client-secret>

  1. Click Get Access Token — the token will appear under the Grant Type section

Additional Setup:

  • API Key: If required, add it under the Headers tab
  • Proxy: You must configure a proxy for authentication; otherwise, the request will fail

Step 2: Download a Document

To download a document from DMS, use the following:

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Name Value

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

cmisselector

properties

filter

cmis:name

BPA Script – Get File Properties:

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    resolveBodyOnly:true,
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
if(fileId){
options.url="/root?objectId=" + fileId.split('folderid:')[1];
}
return options;

 

 

BPA Script – Retrieve File Name:

//retriving file name 
let filename =folderId.objects[0].object.properties["cmis:name"].value;
return filename;

BPA Script – Generate Download URL and Headers:

//Generating the URL and Headers Payload
return{
    url:destination.destinationConfiguration.url+ '/root?objectId='+fileId+'&cmisSelector=content',
    options:{
        headers:{
            Authorization: 'Bearer ' + destination.authTokens[0].value
        }
    }
};

 

Step 3: Get Folder Details 

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Type Name Value

Param

objectId

<folder-object-id>

Param

cmisselector

properties

Param

filter

cmis:objectTypeId

Param

filter

cmis:name

Auth: Pass your access token under Auth tab → Bearer Token

Note: you have to give proxy to for authentication otherwise it will not work 

BPA Script - To get folder details

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    url:'/root?objectId='+objectId.split('folderid:')[1]+'&cmisselector=properties',
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
return options;

Step 4: Upload a Document

Use this when you need to upload a file to DMS via BPA — for example, when receiving a file from a different source.

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Multipart Form):

Key Value

file

<select your file>

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:document

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<file-name>

Query Parameters:

Name Value

cmisaction

createDocument

BPA Script - to Upload File

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?cmisaction=createDocument', 
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: fileName },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: 'cmis:document' },
            { name: 'media', file: filePath }
        ]
};
return options;

Step 5: Create Folder

To create a folder within DM,  use the following

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

createFolder

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:folder

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<folder-name>

BPA Script

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?',
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            {name:'cmisaction', value: 'createFolder'},
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: 'cmis:folder' },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: folderName },
        ]
};
return options;

Step 6: Delete a Document/Folder

To delete a document or folder tree from DMS, use the following:

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

deleteTree

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

unfileObjects

delete

continueOnFailure

true

 

BPA Script - To delete

let objectId = folderId.split('folderid:')[1];
let options = {
    method: 'POST',
    resolveBodyOnly: true,
    responseType: 'json',
    metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
    form:{
        cmisaction:'deleteTree',
        objectId: objectId,
        unfileObjects:'delete',
        continueOnFailure:true
    }
};
return options;

 

Conclusion :

And that's a wrap! By now, you should have a solid understanding of how to work with DMS in the context of SAP Build Process Automation. We covered the full lifecycle — downloading files to your local machine for validation, getting folder details to inspect what's stored, uploading documents from external sources, creating folders to keep things organized, and deleting files once they've served their purpose.

The key takeaway? Always test your DMS APIs  before wiring them into BPA. It's a small step that saves hours of debugging later. Once you're confident the APIs work as expected, integrating them into your automation becomes straightforward.

I hope this guide helps you build more robust and well-tested BPA workflows. If you have questions or run into issues, feel free to drop a comment — happy to help!.

entry.title: Re: Turning “Moments of truth” into lasting impressions with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding

entry.title: NLQ in the real world: what metadata setup made it successful for you?
entry.content:
I recently shared a demo of natural language query (NLQ) and conversational analytics showing the just ask feature in SAP Analytics Cloud and how Joule takes NLQ further into a conversational experience. We also highlighted before that success depends heavily on metadata, including labels for measures, dimensions, and attributes you plan to use. As SAP continues investing in NLQ and conversational analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud, we’d love to learn from your experience.

1) What’s your current status for NLQ (just ask) or conversational analytics (analytical insights in Joule)?

  • Works reliably
  • Works sometimes
  • Not working / not adopted yet
  • Haven’t tried yet

2) What model type are you using?

The just ask feature and so analytical insights in Joule support acquired models in SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere models.

3) What was the single most important metadata edit that improved results?

Examples:

  • Adding/cleaning business-friendly labels for measures/dimensions/attributes
  • Translating labels for multilingual usage
  • Switching to (or refining) a model structure that’s better suited

4) What’s the most common challenge you face?

Examples:

  • Wrong measure/dimension matched
  • Terms not understood
  • Ambiguous business language (“sales”, “revenue”, “margin”, etc.)
  • Time logic confusion (quarters, fiscal periods, etc.)
  • The model feels “too technical” for business users

5) If you’re willing, share one example:

  • The question you asked: e.g., “Show me actuals vs forecast by region.”
  • What you expected:
  • What you got instead:
  • What metadata change fixed it (or didn’t):

Thanks in advance, if we get enough examples, I’ll summarize patterns back to the community (common challenges + top wins) and turn the best practices into a short checklist focused on metadata readiness for NLQ and conversational analytics.

entry.title: Re: NLQ in the real world: what metadata setup made it successful for you?
entry.content:
Your turn!  Here’s a quick template to facilitate sharing your experience. Feel free to answer only the parts you can.

1) Status today

  • Overall experience: reliable / sometimes / not working / haven’t tried
  • Primary users: business analysts / power users / executives / mixed

2) Use case

  • Feature: just ask or analytical insights in Joule (or both)
  • Model type: SAC acquired model / SAP Datasphere model (or other)

3) The single most impactful metadata change:

  • Better labels for measures/dimensions/attributes
  • Adding business-friendly synonyms / terminology alignment
  • Translating labels into the NLQ language we use
  • Cleaning up ambiguous terms (e.g., “sales” vs “revenue”)
  • Other

4) What remains a challenge:

  • Wrong measure/dimension matched
  • Terms not understood / language mismatch
  • Ambiguity (“margin”, “profit”, “net sales”, etc.)
  • Time logic confusion (“Q2”, fiscal periods, YTD)
  • Model is too technical / not business-term friendly
  • Other

5) One example:

  • Question we asked:
  • The answer we expected:
  • What we got instead:
  • What we changed in metadata to improve it:

6) Bonus: if someone asked me “what’s the #1 thing to do first to make NLQ/conversational analytics work,” I’d say: ...

entry.title: How We Built JUNO Joule Agent for Release Testing Automation Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

From my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors release cycles, one thing became very clear early on — automation alone is not enough. What really makes a difference is how intelligently we manage and execute that automation.

In most projects, teams already invest a lot of effort in building automated test scenarios using internal frameworks or QA tools. But the real challenge starts during release time — deciding what to run, when to run it, and how to interpret the results.

I’ve personally seen teams struggle with simple but critical things like:

  • Manually triggering test executions
  • Switching between tools to track execution status
  • Trying to understand raw test results
  • Mapping technical failures back to business impact

Even though automation exists, the process still feels manual and fragmented.

This gap is exactly what led us to build JUNO Joule Agent — to make release testing not just automated, but also intelligent and easy to manage.    

Current Challenge

In most SAP SuccessFactors implementations I’ve worked on, organizations already have:

  • Automated test scenarios built in their release testing frameworks
  • CI/CD pipelines capable of triggering executions

But what’s usually missing is:

  • A simple, business-friendly way to trigger tests
  • Smart selection of relevant test scenarios per release
  • Clear visibility into execution results
  • A guided or conversational way to validate releases

Because of this, even though testing is technically automated, the overall process still depends heavily on manual effort.

Our Approach – JUNO Joule Agent

To solve this problem, we designed JUNO Joule Agent, an AI-powered release testing solution built on SAP Business Technology Platform.

While working on this, my main focus was to ensure the solution feels natural within the SAP ecosystem — secure, scalable, and easy for both technical and business users.

As part of the implementation, we built the solution using SAP Build Process Automation, leveraging Joule Actions, Skills, and Agent capabilities to orchestrate the entire release testing flow. This helped us connect user requests directly with backend automation in a seamless way.

Instead of creating new test scripts again, JUNO reuses existing automated test scenarios from the release testing framework and brings everything into one place.

With this approach, teams can trigger test execution using simple natural language, monitor execution in real time, and analyze results without switching across multiple tools.

It basically adds an intelligent layer on top of existing automation, which is what was missing earlier.

Benefits

1. Centralized Test Dashboard

  • Displays a unified dashboard to execute and monitor test scenarios, along with options to export results.
  • Provides visibility into test scenarios and execution status across different modules.

 

 Figure 1: JUNO Joule Agent showing list of configured Joule Skills, Agents and release testing artifacts

 

2. Intelligent Test Execution via Joule

  • Accepts natural language user requests and identifies the corresponding release test scenario.

  • Triggers test execution directly from the conversation and provides execution summary and monitoring details.

 

Figure 2:  Natural Language-Based Test Execution using Joule with real-time execution summary and monitoring details

 

Figure 3: Real-time test result insights using Joule showing passed and failed scenario counts

 

3. Faster and More Efficient Testing

  • From what I observed during implementation, this significantly reduced repetitive manual testing effort and helped teams complete release cycles faster with fewer errors.

  • It also allowed HR and IT teams to focus more on analysis and improvements instead of spending time on manual execution.

Figure 4: Triggering multiple release test scenarios using Joule with execution status and monitoring details

 

4. Release Highlights Available in the UI

  • Displays SAP SuccessFactors release updates directly within the application.

  • Shows key changes and related information in a structured format for quick reference.

 5. Testing Across SAP SuccessFactors HCM Modules

  • Supports release testing across multiple SAP SuccessFactors modules, including Employee Central, Onboarding, and Recruiting.

  • Retrieves available test scenarios across these modules using Joule.

 

Figure 5:  Conversational retrieval of Employee Central data using Joule with real-time response display

 

Figure 6: Detailed view of Time Off test cases and execution insights retrieved through Joule conversational interface

 

Figure 7: Conversational view of release RCM test cases and associated scenario details using Joule

6. Test Scenario Execution Using Inputs

  • Allows users to run test scenarios for a specific Entity ID instead of executing all scenarios.

  • Executes only the relevant test cases based on the provided input.

 

Figure 8: Input-driven hierarchical test scenario execution using Joule with detailed execution summary

 

Figure 9: Triggering release test scenarios using Joule with execution success status and details

 7. Release Test Coverage Across SAP SuccessFactors Modules

  • Displays the number of automated test scenarios available for each SAP SuccessFactors HCM module.
  • Retrieves test scenario counts across modules using Joule.

 

Figure 10: Conversational retrieval of test scenario counts across all SAP SuccessFactors modules using Joule

 

Figure 11: Real-time insight into average execution time of release test scenarios using Joule

 

Business Value

From my hands-on experience, JUNO brings clear improvements in how release testing is handled:

- Makes the release testing process more structured and easier to manage across different modules
- Significantly reduces manual effort by enabling test execution through natural language and centralized monitoring
- Ensures consistent and repeatable testing, helping avoid missed validations during release cycles
- Minimizes the risk of critical issues by improving test coverage and visibility
- Improves collaboration between HR and IT teams through clear and accessible execution insights
- Runs securely on SAP Business Technology Platform and scales effectively across multiple modules
- In my experience, this also improved overall confidence during release cycles, as teams had better visibility and control over test execution

Conclusion

Release testing in SAP SuccessFactors is critical, but managing it can become complex with frequent updates and multiple dependencies.

From what I’ve seen in real projects, adding an intelligent layer like JUNO Joule Agent completely changes the way teams approach testing. Instead of worrying about tools, scripts, or execution steps, teams can directly focus on validating business scenarios.

For me, the biggest impact was the significant reduction in manual effort, improved clarity across the testing process, and overall smoother and faster release cycles.

This approach not only improves efficiency but also changes how teams think about release testing—from a technical task to a more business-focused validation process.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@Swetha__Balusamy 

Good question — they are different approaches. The Document Grounding service in SAP AI Core is SAP's managed grounding layer that handles document ingestion, chunking, embedding, and retrieval through a configured pipeline without you building the vector store infrastructure yourself. What this article describes is building the RAG pipeline directly on HANA Cloud Vector Store — you manage the embeddings, the table structure, the retrieval logic, and the threshold tuning yourself via Python and the Generative AI Hub SDK.

Both achieve grounding but at different levels of control. Document Grounding is faster to set up and SAP-managed. The HANA Cloud approach gives you full control over chunking strategy, embedding model selection, score thresholds, and re-embedding cadence — which is why this article goes that route for production use cases where those parameters matter. If you're just getting started, Document Grounding is the lower-friction path. If you need production-grade control, build directly on HANA Cloud Vector Store.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@Atul_Joshi85 

Thank you — really glad the production-focused sections landed well. Your question on scale is one of the most important architectural decisions in enterprise RAG and there isn't a universal answer, but here's what I've seen work:

On retrieval accuracy at millions of chunks — cosine similarity with SOURCE_TYPE filtering holds well at scale if your embeddings are consistent (same model, same chunking strategy throughout). Where accuracy degrades is usually not the vector search itself but embedding drift — chunks embedded at different times with different models or different preprocessing. The re-embedding cadence section in the article exists precisely because this is where most production systems quietly degrade.

On unified table vs domain split — I'd recommend splitting by domain, and here's the practical reason: SOURCE_TYPE filtering on a unified table works at moderate scale but becomes a bottleneck when you're combining it with cosine similarity across millions of rows. HANA Cloud's vector engine handles within-partition searches more efficiently than cross-partition filtered searches. Splitting by domain (KB, DDIC, transports, master data) gives you natural partitioning, cleaner re-embedding management per domain, and the ability to tune score thresholds independently — master data typically needs a higher threshold than transport history, for example.

The one case where unified makes sense is when your queries are genuinely cross-domain — if a single user query should retrieve from KB and master data simultaneously. In that case, parallel queries to domain-split tables with result merging at the application layer gives you the best of both.

entry.title: Re: Renovating the ABAP Dictionary: Component-Based Dependency Analysis
entry.content:
Thank you for this information. I think this is a quite important topic!

We experienced some side effects in the last time: the transport of CDS views produced RC 8 of transports, but the views have been activated. Also error messages containing "CBDA" occurred. I also sap SAP Support notes about this problem: 3635323 - Handhabung eines initialen DDL-Source-Typs in der ABAP-Dictionary-Abhängigkeitsberechnung - SAP for Me.

My question is: are there analysis tools when it comes to those error messages of CDS views, that are activated after import? Is there a danger if those views are part of a software component? Can CBDA problems result in installation errors?

Best Regards,
Tobias

entry.title: Re: Characteristic values defined by dependencies not sto...
entry.content:
Please check this:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sales-configuration-display-by-cds-with-table-function-hana-sql-for/ba-p/14381112

the core joins are the following: (however details are described in the blog by link)

  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition seamless integration with Atlassian JIRA - Complete Ultimate Guide
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition seamless integration with Atlassian JIRA - The Ultimate Guide to the Jira Accelerator

Tired of Juggling Data Between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Atlassian JIRA? We’ve Got You Covered!

Managing projects across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Jira Cloud has historically meant manual data entry, misaligned project structures, and time-recording discrepancies. SAP's Jira Integration Accelerator — delivered as a pre-built integration package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub — eliminates exactly this friction. This blog walks you through every configuration step needed to get the integration up and running in your landscape — from system prerequisites to deploying and testing each Integration Flow in SAP Integration Suite.

In the modern agile enterprise, we often see a "tug-of-war" between two critical systems: the System of Project Execution (Jira Cloud) and the System of Record (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition). When these live in silos, the result is predictable: manual data entry, financial discrepancies, and a total lack of real-time visibility.

To bridge this gap, SAP has developed and delivered the Jira Integration Accelerator available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This guide explores the architecture, data flows, and technical "Quick Facts" you need to build a seamless replication journey while maintaining a Clean Core.

The Integration package supports the following business processes:

  • Replicate Projects from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate Project Team Members from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate WBS Elements from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate Time Recordings from Jira Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Business Benefits: Streamlined and automated Project Management processes over numerous touch points

  • Accurate project and employee data in Jira ensures that all Jira tasks can be assigned to valid employees and WBS elements without the need for manual configuration. This alignment guarantees consistency between both systems.
  • Multiple Jira tasks can be assigned to a single WBS element (N:1 relationship), enabling flexible and detailed operational task management in Jira without creating unnecessary WBS elements in S/4. This allows the Project Manager to freely define the tasks required for project execution in Jira, while the Project Accountant maintains a streamlined and efficient WBS structure in S/4.
  • All effort is recorded directly in Jira—the system where the project team members manage and execute their assigned tasks—eliminating the need for them to switch to another tool for time tracking. The recorded hours are automatically transferred to S/4, where activity allocations are generated.
  • These allocations credit the employee’s home cost center and debit the corresponding WBS element. Project costs are calculated based on recorded hours multiplied by the applicable cost rate. Both the effort and the associated costs are reflected in S/4 project reporting, ensuring timely and accurate visibility into project costs and potential budget deviations.
  • For projects billed on a Time and Expense (T&E) basis, the recorded hours are also available for billing purposes.

Quick Facts:

  • Target Group : SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Customers and Partners
  • Positioning : Best of Breed Integration with Jira. Integration accelerator for out of the box process and data synchronization requirements with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Licensing: Standard service plan SAP Integration Suite license, supported with all SAP Business Suite package licenses. Note: SAP Integration Suite Standard service plans include standard edition, enhanced edition, premium edition, premium edition additional instance, SAP Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Free Tier.
  • Out-of-the-box Integration Flow’s for master data & transactional data synchronization with easy customization
  • Unidirectional synchronization from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud:
    • Project & Employee data
    • WBS Elements (Work Breakdown Structure)
  • Unidirectional synchronization from Jira Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for:
    • Time Recording – New Time Recordings, Updated Time Recordings, Deleted Time Recordings

Partner play:

  • Integration Flows for bidirectional synchronization of Time Recording between Jira Cloud & SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Integration flows for integration between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and Jira Cloud
  • UI-based integration for cross-navigation
  • Analytics

In this blog, we’ll start with a high-level overview of what the JIRA integration package offers. Then, we’ll walk you through a step-by-step guide to setting up the package — so you can get your systems connected and running efficiently with minimal effort.

Note : SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with JIRA integration package accelerator isn’t just limited to delivered business scope, its extensible by customers and partners. The package is built with extensibility in mind, so partners can enhance it further to accommodate specific business requirements or tailor it to specific industry needs.

 

SAP provides a variety of pre-built integration accelerators via the SAP Integration Suite, all accessible through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Among these, we’ve developed a dedicated JIRA integration accelerator that enables a seamless connection between your JIRA cloud and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and the JIRA integration adapter is available only for SAP Integration Suite Standard service plans (SAP Integration Suite standard edition, enhanced edition, premium edition, premium edition additional instance, SAP Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Free Tier) . For more information, see SAP Note 3188446.

The  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with Shopify integration package ensures real-time data consistency across both platforms by synchronizing key business objects such as:

  •    Projects
  •    Project Team Members
  •    WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) Elements
  •    Time Recordings

Scope of Integration between JIRA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

The JIRA Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud package is designed to integrate key end-to-end business processes between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and JIRA, enabling smooth, automated data flow and operational consistency across both platforms.

The following core business processes are supported out of the box:

 From SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to JIRA:

  •   Replicate Projects - Automatically sync project headers and basic details to Jira. This ensures that every high-level project defined in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition has a matching project data in Jira, maintaining a single source of truth for project naming and duration.
  •   Replicate Project Team Members - Transfer assigned personnel from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project team directly to the corresponding Jira project. This automates user access and ensures that only authorized team members can log time or manage issues.
  •  Replicate WBS Elements - Synchronize your Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira, typically mapping them to Epics or Features. This allows agile teams to work within the specific financial "buckets" required for corporate budgeting. 

  From JIRA to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  •  Time Recordings - Seamlessly push Jira worklogs back into SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as CATS (Cross-Application Time Sheet) entries. This enables real-time financial tracking, accurate labor costing, and streamlined billing without manual data entry.

Together, the above listed integration flows deliver a robust, automated project management and time tracking experience, reducing manual interventions and ensuring your front-end and back-end systems remain in sync.

 

 How to Set Up the “JIRA Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud  Public Edition” Package

Integrating JIRA with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is now easier than ever — thanks to the pre-built accelerator available on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This step-by-step guide will help you configure and deploy the integration package using SAP Integration Suite, so your systems can work together seamlessly.

 Prerequisites : Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

  •  An SAP Partner Account with access to SAP Integration Suite (via SAP BTP – Business Technology Platform).
  • 🧾  Access to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  •  Access to Atlassian JIRA cloud.
  •   Basic knowledge of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), Integration Suite and JIRA cloud. 
  •   Access to SAP Business Accelerator Hub.
  •  Detailed Configure Guide - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with JIRA Configuration Guide in SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Step 1. Before You Begin — System Access & Authorizations

Before configuring any Integration Flow, confirm you have the required access and authorizations across all three systems.

1.1  System Access Required

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant
  • SAP Integration Suite tenant (Standard or Enterprise Edition)
  • Atlassian Jira Cloud instance with Admin access

1.2  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — Communication Arrangements

A Technical Communication User must be created and assigned to the following three Communication Arrangements. These are mandatory — without them, the Integration Flows cannot call the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition APIs.

 

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP_COM_0027

Workforce Timesheet Integration — required for Time Recording replication (Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

SAP_COM_0308

Enterprise Project Integration — required for Project and WBS Element replication (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

SAP_COM_0008

Business Partner, Customer and Supplier Integration — required for Employee/Team Member sync

For step-by-step instructions on creating Communication Systems, Communication Users, and Communication Arrangements, refer to the SAP Help Portal: help.sap.com → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition  → Communication Management.

1.3  SAP Integration Suite — Role Collections

The user configuring and deploying the Integration Flows must have the following Role Collections assigned in SAP BTP:

  • PI_Integration_Developer
  • PI_Business_Expert
  • PI_Administrator

1.4  Jira Cloud — Adapter Installation

Installing the official Jira Adapter is a mandatory prerequisite before any Integration Flow can be configured or deployed.

 

Action Required: Install the Jira Adapter for SAP Integration Suite before proceeding. Refer to the official 'Jira Adapter for SAP Integration Suite' setup guide on the SAP Help Portal for installation instructions.

Step 2. Configuration in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

This section covers the mandatory configurations required in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant before touching SAP Integration Suite.

2.1  Create the Technical Communication User

A dedicated Technical Communication User is required for the Integration Suite to call OData APIs in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. This user handles all inbound communication from the Integration Suite.

  1. Navigate to the Communication Management section in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Create a new Communication System representing your SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  3. Create a Communication User and assign a secure password — you will need these credentials in the next section.
  4. Create Communication Arrangements for SAP_COM_0027, SAP_COM_0308, and SAP_COM_0008, linking each to the Communication System and User created above.

 

Important: Ensure all Project Team Members in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition have a valid email address in their Business Partner Profile. The Employee Replication flow uses the email address to look up and create matching Jira users — missing emails will cause the flow to skip those team members.

Step 3. Configuration in SAP Integration Suite

3.1  Create SAP S/4HANA Cloud Security Material

After completing the SAP S/4HANA Cloud configuration, the Communication User credentials must be stored as Security Material in SAP Integration Suite. All Integration Flows reference this credential by name.

  1. In SAP Integration Suite, navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs.
  2. Open Security Material.
  3. Click Create → User Credentials.
  4. Configure the credential with the following values and click on Deploy.

Parameter

Value / Description

Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  (use this exact alias — it is referenced across all iFlows)

Description

Credentials for connectivity to SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Type

User Credentials

User

The Communication User created in Step 2 (e.g. jira_accelerator_s4_technical_user)

Password

The password set for the Communication User

 

Tip: The credential name 's4hana_cloud_credentials' is the default alias pre-configured in all Integration Flows. If you use a different name, you must update the Credential Name parameter in every iFlow individually.

Step 4. Configuring and Deploying the Integration Flows

The Jira Accelerator package contains five Integration Flows (iFlows). They must be configured and deployed in the order shown below — each flow depends on the ones before it.

 

Parameter

Value / Description

Step 1

Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements  (supporting flow — no timer)

Step 2

Replicate Single Project Employees from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 3

Replicate Projects from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 4

Replicate WBS Elements from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 5

Replicate Time Records from Jira to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

4.1  Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements

The purpose of this integration flow is to find all team members assigned as resources to the project's WBS elements (work packages). It is only triggered by other integration flows, This is a supporting flow that is called internally by the other iFlows. It is not triggered by a timer — it runs only when invoked by another flow. Configure and deploy it first.

 

Sender (JIRA Integration Adapter)

Parameter

Value / Description

Adapter Type

ProcessDirect

Address

/findAllWorkersAssignedToWBSElements

 

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  (your tenant API URL)  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Proxy Type

Internet  (default)  — Optional

CSRF Protected

 Keep selected (default)  — Optional

Reuse Connection

 Keep selected (default)  — Optional

 

Once configured, Save and Deploy this flow before proceeding to any other iFlow.

4.2  Replicate Single Project Employees (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Project Team Members from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira. It runs on a schedule and creates Jira users for each team member — matching by email address. The Jira User ID is then stored against the Business Partner record in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for bidirectional mapping.

 

Prerequisites

  • Deploy the 'Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements' flow (Step 4.1)
  • All Project Team Members must have a valid email address in their SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Business Partner Profile

Timer

Parameter

Value / Description

Schedule

Configure based on business needs (e.g. Daily, On Deployment for initial load)

Sender (ProcessDirect)

Parameter

Value / Description

Address

/replicateSingleEmployeeToJira

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  (alias of your Jira API Token credential)  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Project Name

The SAP Project ID to sync employees for (e.g. TEST101)  — Mandatory

Jira Products For Employee

Comma-separated Jira product keys (e.g. jira-software)  — Mandatory

Default Initial Request Timestamp

1970-01-01T00:00:00Z  (first run fetches all; delta used thereafter)

Exception Logging

true  (default — recommended to keep enabled)

Customer Pre-Exit Flow Enabled

false  (default — set to true only if using custom pre-processing)

Customer Post-Exit Flow Enabled

false  (default — set to true only if using custom post-processing)

 

Bidirectional User Mapping: Once this flow runs successfully, the Jira User ID is stored as a Note against the Business Partner's default email address in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. To verify, navigate to the Business Partner record → Address Details → Additional Addresses tab.

4.3  Replicate Projects (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Enterprise and Professional Services Projects from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira. It creates new Jira projects for each SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project and keeps them updated on subsequent runs. The Jira Project Key is written back to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Project Description field (prefixed with 'JK:') for bidirectional traceability.

 

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Deploy the 'Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements' flow (Step 4.1)
  • Deploy the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact
  • All Project Team Members must have valid email addresses in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Custom Project Filter

Optional — narrow down projects (e.g. and Project eq 'TEST101')

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Project Type Key

business / software / service_desk  — Mandatory

Jira Project Template Key

e.g. com.atlassian.jira-core-project-templates:jira-core-simplified-project-management  — Mandatory

Jira Preferred Project Role for User

Member  (default) — role assigned to team members  — Mandatory

Jira Default Project Role for User

Administrator  (fallback if preferred role unavailable)  — Mandatory

Project Manager Role Type

YP_RL_0001  (default SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project manager role)  — Mandatory

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Create Project Customer Exit Enabled

false  (default — enable only for custom project creation logic)

 

Jira Project Type Keys & Templates: The guide includes a full reference table of all default Jira Project Type Keys (business, software, service_desk) and their corresponding Template Keys. Match the Project Type Key and Template Key carefully — a mismatch will cause project creation to fail.

Customer Exit: To inject custom logic into project replication, enable 'Create Project Customer Exit Enabled' and configure the CustomerExit_Create_Project receiver. The final step of any custom logic must call the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact.

4.4  Replicate WBS Elements (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition into Jira as custom issue field options. This is what financially anchors every Jira ticket to a real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition cost object. The behaviour differs slightly between Team-Managed and Company-Managed Jira projects — see the important note below.

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Projects must already be replicated using the Replicate Projects flow (Step 4.3)
  • Deploy the security artifacts required for the Integration Content

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Custom Project Filter

Optional — e.g. and Project eq 'TEST101'

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Log Exception Message

true  (default)

HTTP Session Reuse

onExchange  (default)

 

Team-Managed Projects — Manual Step Required: The Jira Cloud API does not currently support automated assignment of custom fields to Team-Managed projects. After this flow runs, you must manually add the WBS Elements custom field to each Team-Managed project:

  1. Go to Project Settings → Fields → Add field

  2. Search for 'WBS Elements' and select the field matching your project name

  3. Click 'Add 1 field', then open each Work Type and drag the field to the desired position on the issue screen

Company-Managed Projects: No manual steps required — the custom field is assigned to project screens automatically.

4.5  Replicate Time Records (Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

This is the most impactful flow in the package — and the one developers interact with every day without ever knowing it. Every Jira worklog entry (new, updated, or deleted) is automatically synchronized to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet with the correct Activity Type and WBS Element. This is a fully change-aware, lifecycle-managed sync.

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Projects must be replicated (Step 4.3)
  • WBS Elements must be replicated (Step 4.4)
  • All relevant Jira users must be mapped to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Business Partners (Step 4.2)

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

Activity Type

T001  (default — maps Jira worklogs to this SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Activity Type; update to match your configuration)  — Optional

Working Hours Per Day

8  (default — must match 'Working hours per day' in Jira settings)  — Optional

Working Week

MON,TUE,WED,THU,FRI  (must match 'Working days per week' in Jira; adjust for 4-day week)  — Optional

SAP S4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Custom Project Filter

Optional — narrow down which projects' time records are processed

Log Exception Message

true  (default — recommended)

 

Working Hours Alignment: The Working Hours Per Day and Working Week parameters must match exactly what is configured in your Jira Cloud instance under Time Tracking settings. A mismatch will cause time durations to be calculated incorrectly when transferred to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet.

Step 5. Monitoring & Operations

Once all five Integration Flows are deployed in SAP Integration suite, monitor them via the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite — Monitor → Integrations → Message Processing Logs: Check the status of each iFlow execution, inspect message payloads, and review error details for failed runs.
  • Exception Logging: With Exception Logging enabled (default: true), any errors are attached as message artifacts in the Integration Suite monitoring — making root cause analysis significantly faster.

5.1  Verifying the Integration End-to-End

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira (Projects): After deploying Step 4.3, trigger the flow manually and verify the project appears in Jira with the correct name, team members, and 'JK:' key appended to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Project Description.
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira (WBS Elements): After deploying Step 4.4, check that the WBS custom field appears on Jira issues with the correct options populated from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  • Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Time Recording): Log a worklog entry against a Jira issue with a WBS Element selected. Trigger the Time Recording flow and verify the entry appears in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet with the correct Activity Type, WBS Element, and duration.
  • Update & Delete: Update then delete the Jira worklog and confirm both changes are reflected in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — confirming the change-aware lifecycle management is working correctly.

Step 6. Extending the Integration with Customer Exits

Each Integration Flow supports Pre-Exit and Post-Exit extension points — allowing partners and customers to inject custom business logic before or after the standard processing, without modifying the core Integration Flow.

  • Customer Pre-Exit Flow: Triggered before the standard processing. Use this to modify or enrich the incoming payload before it is processed.
  • Customer Post-Exit Flow: Triggered after the standard processing. Use this for downstream notifications, additional system updates, or logging.
  • Create Project Customer Exit: A dedicated exit point specifically for project creation — allows customization of the project creation payload. The final step of any custom logic must call the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact to ensure the message structure is correct.

To enable an exit, set the relevant 'Customer Pre/Post-Exit Flow Enabled' parameter to 'true' in the iFlow's More tab, and configure the corresponding ProcessDirect receiver address pointing to your custom flow.

Conclusion

With five Integration Flows, three Communication Arrangements, and a handful of configuration parameters, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with Jira Cloud is fully operational — bridging your System of Record and System of Innovation in a Clean Core-compliant, upgrade-stable way.

Developers stay in Jira. Finance gets real-time, accurate data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Project managers see a consistent, synchronized project structure across both platforms. And the whole thing runs on SAP Integration Suite — with no custom code in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition core.

 

Ready to configure your integration?

Download the full SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration
with Jira Configuration Guide on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Jira Reconciliation Application: 

The "Jira Reconciliation" application is a utility application helping organizations reconcile project-related data between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Atlassian Jira.

The application acts as a companion to the SAP S/4HANA integration package helping teams detect and address cross-system inconsistencies.

Features of Jira Reconciliation application: 

  • Orphaned Issue Detection - Identifies Jira issues linked to WBS elements that no longer exist in SAP S/4HANA, preventing effort from being associated with invalid project structures.
  • Missing WBS Assignment Detection - Flags Jira issues with no valid WBS link, ensuring every issue contributes to accurate project cost tracking.
  • Closed WBS Assignment Detection - Highlights Jira issues still linked to completed or closed WBS elements, preventing effort from being logged against inactive cost objects.
  • Inactive Employee Assignment Detection - Detects Jira issues assigned to employees who are no longer active in SAP, keeping resource data current and compliant.
  • Early Warning Visibility - Surfaces reconciliation issues before they propagate into financial reports or delivery dashboards.
  • Improved Cost Transparency - Supports accurate project cost tracking by keeping Jira and SAP S/4HANA in sync.
  • Reduced Manual Reconciliation Effort - Automates detection of cross-system inconsistencies that would otherwise require time-consuming manual comparison.
  • Faster Issue Resolution - Consolidates reconciliation findings in a single view so teams can identify and address problems quickly.

A big shout out to Maciej Fuchs, Amitha Kamath, Ingo Feucht, Christian Serafimov, Jan Patrzalek, Christoph Liebig, Laura Buesch-Kievit, Javit Gellaw, Stephan Hetzer, Achim Kraiss, for their invaluable contribution in realizing the Jira integration accelerator.

entry.title: Re: Need to update Invoice Ref. field by SD billing document no. for credit mem
entry.content: Go to the definition of your credit note in customizing in transaction VOFA. Tick the field: V_TVFK-XVALGS. Help text for this field: Correction Document with Value Date You can use this check box to control how the baseline date in a correction document's journal entry (for example, journal entry of a credit memo) is determined and whether this journal entry references the corrected billing document's journal entry (for example, journal entry of an invoice). Use If this check box is selected, system behavior is as follows: If the reference billing document has not yet been cleared in financial accounting, the baseline date of the reference document's journal entry is used as the baseline date of the correction document's journal entry upon posting. The number of the reference billing document's journal entry is written to the correction document's journal entry to create an association between the two documents, indicating that they belong together. If the reference billing document has already been cleared, the fixed value date of the correction document (for example, the credit memo or invoice cancellation) is used as its baseline date. If the check box is not selected, the fixed value date of the correction document is used as the baseline date in the journal entry. If the correction document doesn't contain a fixed value date, the system instead uses the billing date of the reference billing document plus the additional value days.

entry.title: SAP PPD - scripting in automation sequence
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

has anyone also faced the issue while working with automation sequences as we have to use multiple calculation (Pco function block ) to extract the data from the input request. 

for eg, if i have input like - 

{"Header": {
"Data_Source_ID": "321",
"Transaction Number": "371",
"Message length": "0",
"DataLayoutId": "230",
"DataLayoutVersion": "1",
"UnitResult": "0",
"PalletId": "00001",
"OperationId": "Opr1",
"StationId": "StationName"
}}

and we need to fetch value of station, operation, Pallet, unitResult, transactionnumber and Data source id then i need to have multiple function block just to fetch the value of above tags from the request. 

this will increase the complexity of the PPD as well as increase the count of the function call. 

to reduce the above things, can we not introduce the scripting into the automation sequences? Please let us know if there are any other way to get the values of these params from the structure.

 

Regards,
Ankit Gupta
