entry.title: Reducing MRP Purchase Requisitions Using BOM Substitute Stock in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
This post documents a real implementation of BAdI PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST in S/4HANA Public Cloud to bridge the gap left by the absence of Manufacturer Part Number (MPN) functionality. We show how to make MRP consider substitute material stock when calculating purchase requisitions — reducing unnecessary procurement. 14 test iterations, 10 debug versions, and one SAP incident ticket later, here is everything we learned.

The Business Problem

In manufacturing, a Bill of Materials (BOM) often defines alternative item groups — components that are interchangeable substitutes for each other. For example, a finished product FG02 might use either Raw Material A (primary, priority 1) or Raw Material B (substitute, priority 2).

In classic SAP ERP, Manufacturer Part Number (MPN) functionality handled material substitution during MRP planning. However, MPN is not supported in S/4HANA Public Cloud. This creates a gap: when the primary component has a shortage, MRP generates a full purchase requisition without considering that a substitute material might already have sufficient stock sitting in the warehouse.

Our scenario:

Material Role Stock Dependent Requirement Shortage

TD_LQ_ROH_APrimary (Priority 1)200 PC-450 PC250 PC
TD_LQ_ROH_BSubstitute (Priority 2)200 PC0None

Without intervention, MRP generates a purchase requisition for 250 PC of ROH_A — completely ignoring the 200 PC of ROH_B sitting unused in the warehouse. With substitute stock considered, the purchase requisition should be only 50 PC.

Before MRP run on single material TD_LQ_FG02 :

After MRP run:

The Solution Approach

Since MPN is unavailable in S/4HANA Public Cloud, we use the BAdI PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST — a developer extensibility enhancement point that lets you modify the stock/requirements list during MRP planning. This BAdI is implemented as an AMDP (ABAP-Managed Database Procedure), meaning all logic is written in SQLScript and executes directly on the HANA database.

The strategy is straightforward:

  1. When MRP processes the primary material (ROH_A): Add a virtual supply element (+200 PC) representing the available substitute stock → reduces the purchase requisition from 250 to 50.
  2. When MRP processes the substitute material (ROH_B): Add a virtual demand element (-200 PC) to reserve that stock → prevents it from appearing as excess inventory.

Expected result after BAdI:

Material Stock BAdI Element Dep. Requirement Available Purchase Req.

ROH_A200+200 (IndReq supply)-450-5050 PC (was 250)
ROH_B200-200 (IndReq demand)00 PC (stock reserved) 

Prerequisites and Setup

System: S/4HANA Public Cloud (any version supporting developer extensibility)

BOM Configuration: The finished good (FG02) must have a BOM where both components share the same Alternative Item Group with different priorities:

Component Alt. Item Group Priority

TD_LQ_ROH_A011 (primary)
TD_LQ_ROH_B012 (substitute)

Custom CDS View — Z_BOM_ALT_GROUP_VIEW:

This view joins BOM header and item data to expose the alternative group relationships:

 
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'BOM Alternative Group View for AMDP'

define view entity Z_BOM_ALT_GROUP_VIEW as select from I_MaterialBOMLink as bom
  inner join I_BillOfMaterialItemBasic as item
    on  item.BillOfMaterial         = bom.BillOfMaterial
    and item.BillOfMaterialCategory = bom.BillOfMaterialCategory
{
  key item.BillOfMaterialComponent  as ComponentMaterial,
  key bom.Plant                     as Plant,
  key item.BillOfMaterial           as BomNumber,
      bom.BillOfMaterialVariant     as BomAlternative,
      item.BillOfMaterialItemCategory as ItemCategory,
      item.AlternativeItemGroup     as AlternativeGroup,
      item.AlternativeItemPriority  as Priority,
      bom.Material                  as HeaderMaterial
}
where
  item.AlternativeItemGroup != ''

The Implementation

AMDP Class — ZCL_PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST

 
CLASS zcl_pph_supply_demand_list DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_pph_supply_demand_list.
    INTERFACES if_amdp_marker_hdb.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_pph_supply_demand_list IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_pph_supply_demand_list~modify_supply_demand_list
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING z_bom_alt_group_view.

    DECLARE lt_add TABLE LIKE :ct_supplydemanditemlist;
    DECLARE lv_material NVARCHAR(40);
    DECLARE lv_plant    NVARCHAR(4);

    -- Step 1: Identify the current material being processed
    SELECT Material INTO lv_material FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__ LIMIT 1;
    SELECT MRPPlant INTO lv_plant    FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__ LIMIT 1;

    -- Step 2: Check if current material is a PRIMARY
    lt_substitutes = SELECT alt.ComponentMaterial AS SubMaterial,
                       CAST(alt.Priority AS INTEGER) AS SubPriority
                     FROM z_bom_alt_group_view AS pr
                     INNER JOIN z_bom_alt_group_view AS alt
                       ON  alt.Plant            = pr.Plant
                       AND alt.BomNumber        = pr.BomNumber
                       AND alt.AlternativeGroup = pr.AlternativeGroup
                       AND alt.ComponentMaterial != pr.ComponentMaterial
                       AND CAST(alt.Priority AS INTEGER) > CAST(pr.Priority AS INTEGER)
                     WHERE pr.ComponentMaterial = :lv_material
                       AND pr.Plant             = :lv_plant;

    IF EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM :lt_substitutes) THEN

      -- PRIMARY BRANCH: Add supply from substitute stock
      lt_stock_source = SELECT 'TD_LQ_ROH_B' AS Material, '1320' AS Plant,
                               CAST(200 AS DECIMAL(31,3)) AS TotalStock
                        FROM dummy;

      lt_sub_total = SELECT CAST(COALESCE(SUM(stk.TotalStock), 0) AS DECIMAL(31,3))
                            AS SubTotal
                     FROM :lt_substitutes AS sub
                     INNER JOIN :lt_stock_source AS stk
                       ON stk.Material = sub.SubMaterial
                       AND stk.Plant = :lv_plant;

      lt_shortage = SELECT
                      CAST(GREATEST(
                        COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN MRPElementType = '-'
                                     THEN ABS(MRPElementOpenQuantity) ELSE 0 END), 0)
                        - COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN MRPElementType = '+'
                                       THEN MRPElementOpenQuantity ELSE 0 END), 0),
                      0) AS DECIMAL(31,3)) AS Shortage
                    FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__;

      lt_offset = SELECT CAST(GREATEST(LEAST(t.SubTotal, s.Shortage), 0)
                         AS DECIMAL(31,3)) AS OffsetQty
                  FROM :lt_sub_total AS t
                  CROSS JOIN :lt_shortage AS s
                  WHERE t.SubTotal > 0 AND s.Shortage > 0;

      IF EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM :lt_offset WHERE OffsetQty > 0) THEN

        INSERT INTO :lt_add(MRPElementAvailyOrRqmtDate, MRPElementOpenQuantity,
                            MRPElementCategory, MRPElementType, MRPAvailability)(
          SELECT TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYYMMDD'),
                 OffsetQty, 'PP', '+', 'X'
          FROM :lt_offset );

        UPDATE :lt_add SET ProcessingKey          = 'A',
                            MRPPlanningSegmentType = '02',
                            MRPPlanningSegment     = '',
                            MRPPlant               = :lv_plant,
                            MRPArea                = :lv_plant,
                            Material               = :lv_material;
      END IF;

    ELSE

      -- Step 3: Check if current material is a SUBSTITUTE
      lt_primaries = SELECT pr.ComponentMaterial AS PrimaryMaterial
                     FROM z_bom_alt_group_view AS alt
                     INNER JOIN z_bom_alt_group_view AS pr
                       ON  pr.Plant            = alt.Plant
                       AND pr.BomNumber        = alt.BomNumber
                       AND pr.AlternativeGroup = alt.AlternativeGroup
                       AND pr.ComponentMaterial != alt.ComponentMaterial
                       AND CAST(pr.Priority AS INTEGER) < CAST(alt.Priority AS INTEGER)
                     WHERE alt.ComponentMaterial = :lv_material
                       AND alt.Plant             = :lv_plant;

      IF EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM :lt_primaries) THEN

        -- SUBSTITUTE BRANCH: Add demand to reserve this material's stock
        lt_stock_source2 = SELECT CAST(200 AS DECIMAL(31,3)) AS DemandQty
                           FROM dummy
                           WHERE :lv_material = 'TD_LQ_ROH_B'
                             AND :lv_plant = '1320';

        IF EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM :lt_stock_source2 WHERE DemandQty > 0) THEN

          INSERT INTO :lt_add(MRPElementAvailyOrRqmtDate, MRPElementOpenQuantity,
                              MRPElementCategory, MRPElementType, MRPAvailability)(
            SELECT TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYYMMDD'),
                   DemandQty, 'PP', '-', 'X'
            FROM :lt_stock_source2 );

          UPDATE :lt_add SET ProcessingKey          = 'A',
                              MRPPlanningSegmentType = '02',
                              MRPPlanningSegment     = '',
                              MRPPlant               = :lv_plant,
                              MRPArea                = :lv_plant,
                              Material               = :lv_material;
        END IF;
      END IF;

    END IF;

    -- Step 4: Return original rows + any added rows
    ct_supplydemanditemlist =
      SELECT * FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__
      UNION ALL SELECT * FROM :lt_add;

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

How It Works — Step by Step

  1. Material identification: The BAdI fires once per material in the MRP run. We read the current material from the input table's first row.

  2. Role detection via BOM view: A self-join on Z_BOM_ALT_GROUP_VIEW determines whether the current material has substitutes (it's a primary) or has a primary (it's a substitute). Materials not in any alternative group (like FG02) get clean passthrough.

  3. Primary material — add supply: We calculate MIN(substitute_stock, shortage) and add a positive MRP element. This tells MRP that additional supply is available, reducing the purchase requisition.

  4. Substitute material — add demand: We add a negative MRP element equal to the substitute's stock, reserving it so it doesn't appear as excess inventory.

  5. Passthrough: Original rows are always returned via UNION ALL, preserving all standard MRP data.

Key Breakthroughs and Hard-Won Lessons

This implementation required 14 test iterations and 10 debug versions before reaching a working solution. Below are the most time-consuming problems and their solutions.

1. ProcessingKey — The Silent Killer

Time spent: Multiple test iterations (Tests 1–9) before discovery.

Problem: Every modification to the supply/demand list was silently ignored. No error, no warning — the BAdI appeared to fire correctly, but MD04 showed unchanged data.

Root cause: The BAdI caller only processes rows that have ProcessingKey set:

ProcessingKey Meaning

'A'Added row (new MRP element)
'C'Changed row (modified existing element)
'D'Deleted row (remove existing element)
(empty)Ignored — treated as unchanged

Without ProcessingKey = 'A', your added rows are silently discarded. This is not documented in the BAdI interface definition. We only discovered it after SAP's development team responded to an incident we raised, pointing us to the example class CL_EX_PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST.

Lesson: Always set ProcessingKey on every row you add or modify. For adding new supply/demand elements, use 'A'.

Important: In our testing, only ProcessingKey = 'A' (Add) reliably worked. ProcessingKey = 'C' (Change) had no effect when modifying existing BD or BS rows — even when following SAP's documented DELETE + INSERT pattern exactly. Our solution was to add new elements rather than modify existing ones.

2. The INSERT + UPDATE Two-Step Pattern

Problem: In an OPTIONS READ-ONLY AMDP, you cannot use a simple inline SELECT with all columns to construct a new row.

Solution: Use a two-step pattern — first INSERT the data columns, then UPDATE the metadata columns:

 
-- Step 1: INSERT data columns via SELECT from dummy
INSERT INTO :lt_add(MRPElementAvailyOrRqmtDate, MRPElementOpenQuantity,
                    MRPElementCategory, MRPElementType, MRPAvailability)(
  SELECT '20260424', CAST(200 AS DECIMAL(31,3)),
         'PP', '+', 'X'
  FROM dummy );

-- Step 2: UPDATE metadata columns
UPDATE :lt_add SET ProcessingKey          = 'A',
                    MRPPlanningSegmentType = '02',
                    MRPPlanningSegment     = '',
                    MRPPlant               = '1320',
                    MRPArea                = '1320',
                    Material               = 'TD_LQ_ROH_A';

This matches the pattern used in SAP's own example implementation. Do not attempt to set all columns in a single INSERT statement.

3. StorageLocation Is NOT a Column

Problem: Adding StorageLocation to the INSERT column list caused the entire output to be silently empty — no error, no dump, just zero rows returned to the caller.

Root cause: StorageLocation is not a column in the ct_supplydemanditemlist table type. Including it doesn't cause a syntax error, but it corrupts the output in a way that the caller silently discards everything.

Lesson: Only use columns that actually exist in the ct_supplydemanditemlist structure. The key columns that work are:

  • Material, MRPPlant, MRPArea
  • MRPElementAvailyOrRqmtDate, MRPElementOpenQuantity
  • MRPElementCategory, MRPElementType, MRPAvailability
  • ProcessingKey, MRPPlanningSegmentType, MRPPlanningSegment

4. SAP Stock CDS Views Return 0 Rows from AMDP

Time spent: DEBUG 5–7 were entirely dedicated to this problem.

Problem: I_MaterialStock_2 and our custom wrapper Z_UNRESTRICTED_STOCK_VIEW return zero rows when queried from within the AMDP procedure, even with the USING clause properly declared.

Investigation: We created diagnostic versions that counted rows at different filter levels:

Filter Applied Rows Returned

With plant + material filter0 rows
With plant filter only0 rows
No filter at all0 rows
BOM view (I_MaterialBOMLink)Data returned normally

Root cause: I_MaterialStock_2 has @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #MANDATORY and is an analytical cube view (@analytics: { dataCategory: #CUBE }). These characteristics make it inaccessible from AMDP procedures, likely due to the ABAP Extension Check restrictions in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Workaround for PoC: Hardcode the substitute stock values. For a production implementation, options include:

  • Read stock quantities in the ABAP layer before the AMDP call, then pass them via an additional importing parameter
  • Use a Table UDF (BY DATABASE FUNCTION) which has looser Extension Check restrictions

5. The BAdI Fires for ALL Materials — Including the Finished Good

Time spent: Test 15 was specifically created to diagnose this.

Problem: When making the logic dynamic, unexpected rows appeared on the finished good (FG02). The BAdI fires not only for raw material components (ROH_A, ROH_B) but also for the parent finished good.

Solution: The dynamic version must handle three cases:

Material Role Detected By Action

Primary (has substitutes)Alt group join: substitutes foundAdd supply element
Substitute (has a primary)Alt group join: primary foundAdd demand element
Other (FG02 or not in any alt group)Falls through both IF branchesClean passthrough

The BOM view self-join naturally handles this: materials not found in Z_BOM_ALT_GROUP_VIEW fall through both IF branches and receive pure passthrough — no extra rows added.

6. Scalar SELECT INTO with Multiple Columns Does Not Work

Problem: In OPTIONS READ-ONLY AMDP, a single SELECT returning multiple columns into separate scalar variables fails:

 
-- THIS DOES NOT WORK in OPTIONS READ-ONLY AMDP:
SELECT Material, MRPPlant INTO lv_material, lv_plant
FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__ LIMIT 1;

Solution: Use separate SELECT statements, one per scalar variable:

 
SELECT Material INTO lv_material FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__ LIMIT 1;
SELECT MRPPlant INTO lv_plant    FROM :ct_supplydemanditemlist__in__ LIMIT 1;

Dead Ends and Wrong Turns

During the weeks-long investigation, we explored several approaches that ultimately did not work. Documenting them here to save others from the same detours.

Dead End 1: Plain ABAP Instead of AMDP

The interface IF_PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST requires the method to be implemented as BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT. A plain ABAP implementation cannot be activated.

Dead End 2: Modifying Existing Rows (ProcessingKey = 'C')

Despite following SAP's documented DELETE + INSERT pattern with ProcessingKey = 'C', changes to existing BD (dependent requirement) and BS (stock) rows had zero effect across multiple test iterations (Tests 7, 9, 11, 12). The working approach is to add new elements with ProcessingKey = 'A'.

Dead End 3: Custom CDS View for Stock Lookups

Z_UNRESTRICTED_STOCK_VIEW wrapping I_MaterialStock_2 suffered the same zero-rows problem. The underlying view is inaccessible from AMDP regardless of wrapping. We spent significant time on DEBUG 5–7 isolating this, testing different WHERE clauses, removing filters — always 0 rows.

Dead End 4: Single-Step INSERT with All Columns

The ABAP Extension Check in S/4HANA Public Cloud restricts certain DML patterns in OPTIONS READ-ONLY procedures. The two-step pattern (INSERT data columns from a SELECT, then UPDATE metadata columns) is the only reliable approach.

Dead End 5: Z-Table for Stock Snapshots (ZSTOCK_SNAPSHOT)

Idea: Since SAP's analytical stock views return 0 rows from AMDP (see Lesson #4 above), create a physical
transparent Z-table `ZSTOCK_SNAPSHOT` (fields: `CLIENT`, `MATERIAL`, `PLANT`, `STOCK`, `REFRESHED_AT`) as a
pre-computed stock cache. A scheduled ABAP report `ZCL_REFRESH_STOCK_SNAPSHOT` would run before each MRP run — on the
application server, where analytical CDS views *are* accessible — read unrestricted stock from `I_MaterialStock_2`,
and write the results into the Z-table. The AMDP would then read this snapshot at MRP time.

Why it's a dead end for this PoC: The approach is architecturally sound and would work in production. However, it
introduces operational complexity (a scheduled job that must run before every MRP run, table maintenance, staleness
risk) that distracts from the core BAdI pattern this blog post demonstrates. For the proof-of-concept, the stock value
(200 PC for TD_LQ_ROH_B) is hardcoded directly in the SQLScript to keep the focus on the BAdI logic itself.

Why this matters: This is likely the most practical production path. The key insight is that AMDP cannot access
SAP's analytical cube views — so any real stock data must be staged into a physical table beforehand. Understanding
*why* (analytical engine runs on the app server, AMDP runs on the DB layer) saves significant debugging time.

Summary

The BAdI PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST provides a viable workaround for the missing MPN functionality in S/4HANA Public Cloud. By adding virtual supply and demand elements during MRP planning, we can effectively tell MRP to consider substitute material stock when calculating purchase requisitions.

The implementation is conceptually simple — the difficulty lies entirely in the AMDP constraints. The key rules to remember:

  1. Always set ProcessingKey = 'A' on added rows — without it, your changes are silently ignored
  2. Use the INSERT + UPDATE two-step pattern — single-step construction fails
  3. Never include StorageLocation in your column lists — it silently kills all output
  4. SAP stock CDS views are inaccessible from AMDP — plan an alternative data source
  5. The BAdI fires for every material in the MRP run, not just components — handle the finished good with clean passthrough
  6. Use separate scalar SELECTs for each variable — multi-column SELECT INTO does not work

For a production scenario, the hardcoded stock values would need to be replaced with a proper stock lookup mechanism — potentially via a Table UDF helper function or by extending the BAdI interface. But the core pattern of adding virtual supply/demand elements to offset BOM substitutes is proven and ready for adaptation.

The features covered in this article are based on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition 2602, please refer to the latest information for changes in subsequent versions.

Hope you LIKE it if it addresses your issue. After that, please feel free to comment after following my account and I will reply ASAP.

entry.title: Re: SAP PPD - scripting in automation sequence
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

we have raised the CIR for the same - 

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/372478/?section=sectionDetails

Regards,
Ankit Gupta

entry.title: Re: Agent determination by badi for non po invoices
entry.content:
Hello @MP_77,

an example can be found within the app Custom Logic in the Code Editor:

 

 

Sample Code

 
*    DATA:
*      ls_badi_approver     TYPE if_mrm_workflow_agents=>bd_mmpur_s_badi_approver,
*      lt_badi_approver     TYPE if_mrm_workflow_agents=>bd_mmpur_t_badi_approver,
*      ls_previous_approver TYPE if_mrm_workflow_agents=>bd_mmpur_s_previous_approver,
*      ls_new_approver      TYPE if_mrm_workflow_agents=>bd_mmpur_s_badi_approver.
*
** define the list of BADI approvers
**  -----  Approval Level 1
*    ls_badi_approver-businessuser = 'MMPURMANAG1'.
*    ls_badi_approver-approvallevel = 1.
*    APPEND ls_badi_approver TO lt_badi_approver.
**  -----  Approval Level 2
*    ls_badi_approver-businessuser = 'MMPURMANAG2'.
*    ls_badi_approver-approvallevel = 2.
*    APPEND ls_badi_approver TO lt_badi_approver.
**  -----  Approval Level 3
*    ls_badi_approver-businessuser = 'MMPURMANAG3'.
*    ls_badi_approver-approvallevel = 3.
*    APPEND ls_badi_approver TO lt_badi_approver.
**  -----  Approval Level 4
*    ls_badi_approver-businessuser = 'MMPURMANAG4'.
*    ls_badi_approver-approvallevel = 4.
*    APPEND ls_badi_approver TO lt_badi_approver.
*
**  remove the previous approvers from the list of BAdI approvers
*    LOOP AT previousapproverlist INTO ls_previous_approver.
*      READ TABLE lt_badi_approver INTO ls_badi_approver
*                              WITH KEY businessuser = ls_previous_approver-businessuser.
*      CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
*      DELETE lt_badi_approver WHERE approvallevel = ls_badi_approver-approvallevel.
*    ENDLOOP.
*
**  determine the next approval level and appropriate approvers
*    READ TABLE lt_badi_approver INTO ls_badi_approver INDEX 1.
*    LOOP AT lt_badi_approver INTO ls_new_approver
*                WHERE approvallevel = ls_badi_approver-approvallevel.
*      APPEND ls_new_approver-businessuser TO approverlist.
*    ENDLOOP.
 
 

Explanation

This sample code implements a fixed, sequential approval chain for supplier invoice workflows. It defines four hard‑coded approvers and removes those who have already approved from the list.
The workflow then selects the next lowest remaining approval level and assigns only that approver to the next workflow step. As a result, approvals happen one after another, with no parallel or rule‑based logic.
 
Best regards,
Kerisha

entry.title: Re: How to process Mass retirement of assets in SAP S4 Ha...
entry.content:
Dear @Cheng_Huang58,

For standard manual postings the relevant Public Cloud app is Post Asset Retirement F7107. SAP states in the app documentation that this app is used for asset retirements, and for the retirement of multiple assets the corresponding Fixed Asset Post Asset Retirement API should be used

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/c77245b8f1c34fcf99e3a88b072925f1.html

For mass processing the recommended Public Cloud approach is therefore to use the released API Fixed Asset Post Asset Retirement, service API_FIXEDASSETRETIREMENT_G4BA. SAP confirms that this API covers asset retirement postings with and without revenue and that the transactions correspond to those from app F7107

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/5bcca49998834de2b5533c36602e52a2/2b3aafb1a2df4e6fa8610fe55a375449.html

The app Mass Processing of Assets should not be treated as the general AR01 / AR31 replacement. SAP explains that this mass-processing app is part of the Oil & Gas solution scope and is available only when the relevant scope/licensing is in place, specifically scope item 4ON and the associated Joint Venture Accounting 3F7 context

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3219541

The reason the classic AR01 / AR31 approach is not available in the same way is the Public Cloud fit-to-standard model. These are classic Asset Accounting worklist transactions from the on-premise/private-edition model while Public Edition provides the standard Fiori posting app for individual processing and a released API for mass/automated processing

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Purchase Order Condition Not Included in Total
entry.content:
Based on your current design PO layout showing total PO price excluding tolling cost where may be tolling cost with tolling condition type charged by different vendor or tracked differently with a G/L account! 

As mentioned by you, the standard conditions (e.g., base price, freight) only considering for PO TOTAL Price while the tolling cost behaves differently thus  tolling cost with tolling condition type have its own accounting key where upon posting different G/L account posted other than inventory account! Refer the MM Pricing procedure associated for your PO  and check how tolling cost with tolling condition type designed with its placing(FROM and TO) and if you wanted add tolling cost with tolling condition type to TOTAL PO PRICE(effective price) then re-design your tolling cost with tolling condition type in your MM Pricing procedure along with set up for  tolling condition type.

entry.title: Re: Create supplier invoice app and import supplier invoi...
entry.content:
Hello @charlene2

Fair point, yes, if Create Incoming Invoices already flows to DRC, the company code source type activation is likely fine. Then the like reason for what you see is that Create Supplier Invoice and Import Supplier Invoice are posting a different accounting document type or different invoice object flow, and that exact posting result is not mapped for DRC even though another FI invoice app is.

You should then check the following:

  1. Post one test invoice from each app.
  2. Open the accounting document that results from those posts.
  3. Compare the document type
  4. Then verify whether that exact document type is assigned to Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAC AutoSum Custom Widget — Excel's Status Bar, Native in SAC with Full Code Listing
entry.content:
The SAC AutoSum Custom Widget consists of just two files — widget.json and widget.js. No external libraries, no dependencies, no backend.

The widget.json defines the widget's identity, its configurable properties (colors, locale, which tiles to show), and two methods: clearValues() and addValue(). These are the bridge to the Analytical Application.

The widget.js contains the full logic: value parsing, aggregate calculation, and rendering — all encapsulated in a Web Component with Shadow DOM.

The integration on the SAC side is a single script block in the Table Widget's onSelect event: clear the previous selection, then loop through the selected cells and pass each raw value to the widget via addValue(). The widget handles the rest — live, with every selection change.

One bonus feature: select exactly two cells, and the widget automatically shows a Delta tile — absolute difference and percentage deviation. No extra step, no configuration needed.

Code for: widgest.json

{
  "id": "comdirksacautosum",
  "version": "1.0.4",
  "name": "SAC AutoSum",
  "description": "Zeigt SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX und COUNT der selektierten Tabellenzellen an — analog zur Excel-Statusleiste.",
  "vendor": "SAP",
  "eula": "",
  "license": "",
  "newInstancePrefix": "AutoSumWidget",
  "webcomponents": [
    {
      "kind": "main",
      "tag": "com-dirk-sac-autosum",
      "url": "/widget.js",
      "integrity": "",
      "ignoreIntegrity": true
    }
  ],
  "properties": {
    "locale": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Zahlenformat-Locale (z.B. de-DE, en-US)",
      "default": "de-DE"
    },
    "showSum": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Summe anzeigen",
      "default": true
    },
    "showAvg": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Durchschnitt anzeigen",
      "default": true
    },
    "showMin": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Minimum anzeigen",
      "default": true
    },
    "showMax": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Maximum anzeigen",
      "default": true
    },
    "showCount": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Anzahl anzeigen",
      "default": true
    },
    "showDelta": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "description": "Show difference tile when exactly 2 cells are selected",
      "default": true
    },
    "accentColor": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Akzentfarbe (Trennlinien), z.B. #0070F2",
      "default": "#0070F2"
    },
    "labelColor": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Farbe der Beschriftungen (SUM, AVG, ...), z.B. #6A6D70",
      "default": "#6A6D70"
    },
    "valueColor": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Farbe der Zahlenwerte, z.B. #32363A",
      "default": "#32363A"
    },
    "backgroundColor": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Hintergrundfarbe des Widgets, z.B. #FFFFFF",
      "default": "#F5F6F7"
    }
  },
  "methods": {
    "addValue": {
      "description": "Fuegt einen einzelnen numerischen Wert zur Selektion hinzu. Pro Zelle einmal aufrufen.",
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "value",
          "type": "string",
          "description": "Numerischer Wert (rawValue aus getResultSet)"
        }
      ]
    },
    "clearValues": {
      "description": "Setzt die Selektion zurueck. Vor jeder neuen Selektion aufrufen."
    }
  },
  "events": {
    "onSelectionChange": {
      "description": "Feuert wenn sich die Selektion aendert"
    }
  }
}

 

code for widget.js

(function () {
    'use strict';

    // ── Defaults ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    function defaultProps() {
        return {
            selectedValues: '[]',
            locale: 'de-DE',
            showSum: 'true',
            showAvg: 'true',
            showMin: 'true',
            showMax: 'true',
            showCount: 'true',
            showDelta: 'true',
            accentColor: '#0070F2',
            labelColor: '#6A6D70',
            valueColor: '#32363A',
            backgroundColor: '#F5F6F7'
        };
    }

    // ── Helpers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    function parseValues(jsonStr) {
        if (!jsonStr || jsonStr === '[]') return [];
        try {
            var arr = JSON.parse(jsonStr);
            var result = [];
            for (var i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
                var n;
                if (typeof arr[i] === 'number') {
                    n = arr[i];
                } else {
                    var s = String(arr[i]).trim();
                    var lastDot = s.lastIndexOf('.');
                    var lastComma = s.lastIndexOf(',');
                    if (lastComma > lastDot) {
                        s = s.replace(/\./g, '').replace(',', '.');
                    } else {
                        s = s.replace(/,/g, '');
                    }
                    n = parseFloat(s);
                }
                if (!isNaN(n)) result.push(n);
            }
            return result;
        } catch (e) {
            return [];
        }
    }

    function computeStats(vals) {
        if (!vals || vals.length === 0) return null;
        var sum = 0;
        var min = vals[0];
        var max = vals[0];
        for (var i = 0; i < vals.length; i++) {
            sum += vals[i];
            if (vals[i] < min) min = vals[i];
            if (vals[i] > max) max = vals[i];
        }
        return { sum: sum, avg: sum / vals.length, min: min, max: max, count: vals.length };
    }

    function computeDelta(vals) {
        if (!vals || vals.length !== 2) return null;
        var diff = vals[0] - vals[1];
        var pct = vals[1] !== 0 ? (diff / Math.abs(vals[1])) * 100 : null;
        return { diff: diff, pct: pct };
    }

    function formatNum(n, locale) {
        try {
            return n.toLocaleString(locale, { minimumFractionDigits: 2, maximumFractionDigits: 2 });
        } catch (e) {
            return String(Math.round(n * 100) / 100);
        }
    }

    function buildCSS(props) {
        return '<style>' +
            ':host { display: block; width: 100%; height: 100%; font-family: "72","SAP72",Arial,sans-serif; }' +
            '* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }' +
            '.bar { display: flex; height: 100%; min-height: 56px; max-height: 72px; background: ' + props.backgroundColor + '; border-radius: 6px; overflow: hidden; border: 1px solid #E8EAED; }' +
            '.tile { flex: 1; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; border-right: 1px solid #E8EAED; padding: 0 8px; min-width: 0; cursor: pointer; }' +
            '.tile:last-child { border-right: none; }' +
            '.tile:hover { background: rgba(0,112,242,0.06); }' +
            '.tile-delta { background: rgba(0,112,242,0.04); }' +
            '.tile-delta:hover { background: rgba(0,112,242,0.1); }' +
            '.tile-label { font-size: 11px; color: ' + props.labelColor + '; margin-bottom: 2px; letter-spacing: 0.3px; white-space: nowrap; }' +
            '.tile-value { font-size: 15px; font-weight: 700; color: ' + props.valueColor + '; white-space: nowrap; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; max-width: 100%; }' +
            '.tile-value.idle { color: #B0B3B7; font-weight: 400; }' +
            '.tile-pct { font-size: 10px; color: #6A6D70; margin-top: 2px; white-space: nowrap; }' +
            '.builder-hint { font-size: 11px; color: #6A6D70; background: #E8F3FF; border-top: 1px solid #91C8F6; padding: 6px 12px; text-align: center; }' +
            '.toast { position: fixed; bottom: 24px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); background: #32363A; color: #fff; font-size: 12px; padding: 6px 14px; border-radius: 4px; opacity: 0; pointer-events: none; transition: opacity 0.2s ease; z-index: 9999; white-space: nowrap; }' +
            '.toast.show { opacity: 1; }' +
            '</style>';
    }

    function buildTiles(stats, props, delta) {
        var locale = props.locale || 'de-DE';
        var idle = !stats;
        var tiles = [
            { show: props.showSum,   label: 'Sum',   val: idle ? '—' : formatNum(stats.sum, locale),   pct: null },
            { show: props.showAvg,   label: 'Avg',   val: idle ? '—' : formatNum(stats.avg, locale),   pct: null },
            { show: props.showMin,   label: 'Min',   val: idle ? '—' : formatNum(stats.min, locale),   pct: null },
            { show: props.showMax,   label: 'Max',   val: idle ? '—' : formatNum(stats.max, locale),   pct: null },
            { show: props.showCount, label: 'Count', val: idle ? '—' : String(stats.count),            pct: null }
        ];

        var html = '<div class="bar">';
        for (var i = 0; i < tiles.length; i++) {
            var visible = tiles[i].show === true || tiles[i].show === 'true';
            if (!visible) continue;
            html += '<div class="tile">' +
                '<span class="tile-label">' + tiles[i].label + '</span>' +
                '<span class="tile-value' + (idle ? ' idle' : '') + '">' + tiles[i].val + '</span>' +
                '</div>';
        }

        // Delta tile — only when exactly 2 values selected and showDelta enabled
        var showDelta = props.showDelta === true || props.showDelta === 'true';
        if (showDelta && delta !== null) {
            var diffStr = formatNum(delta.diff, locale);
            var pctStr = delta.pct !== null
                ? formatNum(delta.pct, locale) + ' %'
                : 'n/a';
            html += '<div class="tile tile-delta">' +
                '<span class="tile-label">Delta</span>' +
                '<span class="tile-value">' + diffStr + '</span>' +
                '<span class="tile-pct">' + pctStr + '</span>' +
                '</div>';
        }

        html += '</div>';
        html += '<div class="toast">Copied!</div>';
        return html;
    }

    // ── Runtime Widget ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    class AutoSumWidget extends HTMLElement {
        constructor() {
            super();
            this.attachShadow({ mode: 'open' });
            this._props = null;
            this._values = [];
        }

        connectedCallback() {
            if (!this._props) {
                this._props = defaultProps();
            }
            this._render();
        }

        onCustomWidgetAfterUpdate(changedProperties) {
            if (!this._props) {
                this._props = defaultProps();
            }
            for (var key in changedProperties) {
                if (Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(changedProperties, key)) {
                    this._props[key] = changedProperties[key];
                }
            }
            this._render();
        }

        onCustomWidgetBeforeUpdate(changedProperties) { }

        addValue(value) {
            var n = parseFloat(String(value));
            if (!isNaN(n)) {
                this._values.push(n);
            }
            this._render();
        }

        clearValues() {
            this._values = [];
            this._render();
        }

        _render() {
            var props = this._props || defaultProps();
            var stats = computeStats(this._values);
            var delta = computeDelta(this._values);
            this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = buildCSS(props) + buildTiles(stats, props, delta);
            this._attachCopyHandlers();
        }

        _attachCopyHandlers() {
            var self = this;
            var bar = this.shadowRoot.querySelector('.bar');
            if (!bar) return;
            bar.addEventListener('click', function (e) {
                var tile = e.target.closest('.tile');
                if (!tile) return;
                var valueEl = tile.querySelector('.tile-value');
                if (!valueEl) return;
                var text = valueEl.textContent.trim();
                if (text === '—') return;
                try {
                    navigator.clipboard.writeText(text).then(function () {
                        self._showToast();
                    });
                } catch (err) {
                    var ta = document.createElement('textarea');
                    ta.value = text;
                    document.body.appendChild(ta);
                    ta.select();
                    document.execCommand('copy');
                    document.body.removeChild(ta);
                    self._showToast();
                }
            });
        }

        _showToast() {
            var toast = this.shadowRoot.querySelector('.toast');
            if (!toast) return;
            toast.classList.add('show');
            setTimeout(function () { toast.classList.remove('show'); }, 1500);
        }
    }

    // ── Builder Widget ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    class AutoSumWidgetBuilder extends HTMLElement {
        constructor() {
            super();
            this.attachShadow({ mode: 'open' });
            this._props = null;
        }

        connectedCallback() {
            if (!this._props) {
                this._props = defaultProps();
            }
            this._render();
        }

        onCustomWidgetAfterUpdate(changedProperties) {
            if (!this._props) {
                this._props = defaultProps();
            }
            for (var key in changedProperties) {
                if (Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(changedProperties, key)) {
                    this._props[key] = changedProperties[key];
                }
            }
            this._render();
        }

        onCustomWidgetBeforeUpdate(changedProperties) { }

        _render() {
            var props = this._props || defaultProps();
            var exampleStats = { sum: 42000, avg: 8400, min: 2.46, max: 24.72, count: 5 };
            var html = buildCSS(props) + buildTiles(exampleStats, props, null) +
                '<div class="builder-hint">AAp-Script: AutoSumWidget_1.clearValues() + addValue()</div>';
            this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = html;
        }
    }

    // ── Register ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    customElements.define('com-dirk-sac-autosum', AutoSumWidget);
    customElements.define('com-dirk-sac-autosum-builder', AutoSumWidgetBuilder);

}());

 onSelect Function:

// SAC AAp Script: Table_1.onSelect
// Einfügen im SAC Script-Editor unter Table_1 → onSelect
//
// Überträgt selektierte Tabellenzellen in den Business Calculator (BusinessCalc_1).
// Funktioniert mit Einzel- und Mehrfachselektion.
//
// Voraussetzung: Widget heißt BusinessCalc_1, Table heißt Table_1
// Den Key "Account" ggf. durch den modellspezifischen Dimensions-Key ersetzen.
// Tipp: Key ermitteln mit:
//   var r = Table_1.getDataSource().getResultSet(Table_1.getSelections()[0]);
//   for (var k in r[0]) { Text_1.applyText(k); }

Table_1.onSelect = function() {
    var sel = Table_1.getSelections();
    if (sel.length === 0) { return; }
    for (var i = 0; i < sel.length; i++) {
        var results = Table_1.getDataSource().getResultSet(sel[i]);
        if (results.length > 0) {
            var cell = results[0]["Account"];
            if (cell) {
                BusinessCalc_1.addToTape(cell.rawValue, cell.description);
            }
        }
    }
};


// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// ALTERNATIVE: Generischer Key "@MeasureDimension" (modelunabhängig)
// Funktioniert bei Standard-SAC-Modellen. Falls "Account" nicht passt,
// diesen Block stattdessen verwenden:
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
//
// Table_1.onSelect = function() {
//     var sel = Table_1.getSelections();
//     if (sel.length === 0) { return; }
//     for (var i = 0; i < sel.length; i++) {
//         var results = Table_1.getDataSource().getResultSet(sel[i]);
//         if (results.length > 0) {
//             var cell = results[0]["@MeasureDimension"];
//             if (cell) {
//                 BusinessCalc_1.addToTape(cell.rawValue, cell.description);
//             }
//         }
//     }
// };

One thing worth noting for the AAp-Script integration: the getResultSet() call requires a dimension key to read the cell value. In the example script, this key is "Account" — but this is model-specific and may differ in your data model.

If you're unsure which key to use, the script includes a small helper snippet: run it once, and it prints all available keys for the selected cell. As a model-independent alternative, "@MeasureDimension" works in most standard SAC models.

The Deployment:

To deploy the widget in SAC, create a ZIP file containing both widget.json and widget.js — both files must sit in the root of the ZIP, not in a subfolder. Then upload into SAC and have fun.

Let me know what you made out of it. Like to hear about enhancements and such.

entry.title: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud (C4C) 2605 Pre-Release Briefing
entry.content:
Hi All,

SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud (C4C) Pre-Release Briefing presentation for the upcoming 2605 (May 2026) release is now available. We have some key innovations to be shared as part of SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud 2605 Release.

Platform & Integration:

Mandatory Adoption – Convert Embedded SAC Stories to Optimized Design Experience (ODE) Format

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) has introduced Optimized Design Experience, which enables content within an Embedded SAC story to load quickly. Existing stories that are in ‘classic’ mode will remain in the classic format until they are converted to ODE format. Please follow the simple steps mentioned in our help documentation here to convert your existing stories into the ODE format. The help documentation also contains links from SAC documentation on this topic.

Please note that SAC plans to deprecate the classic format by the end of Q2 2026. Hence, customers are advised to perform the conversion of their classic Embedded SAC stories before the mentioned timeframe.

Transition Tools:

  • Readiness Check Tool (RCT) | Shows installed Add-Ons in V1 tenant - The “Installed Add-Ons” tab shows Add-Ons applied in the V1 tenant that the RCT is running on.
  • Data Transfer Tool (DTT) | Introducing ”Count Summary” in Org Structures - Introducing “Count Summary” with “Org Structures” to classify transfer data instances within business object.
  • Data Transfer Tool (DTT) | Key User Extensibility field Transfer in BP Relationships - Business Partner (BP) Relationships support Transfer of Key User Extensibility Field transfer in DTT.
  • Data Transfer Tool (DTT) | Transfer Code List Mapping as part of BC Transfer - “Transfer All BC” now includes Code List Mapping.

Feeds | Integration with SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition - As an Administrator, you have an option to use SAP Jam (until 31st December 2026 due to deprecation of SAP Jam collaboration) or SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition for collaboration via Feeds. This was communicated earlier in the SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud 2511 Release Briefing. Kindly plan to move from SAP Jam and start using SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition for collaboration via Feeds based on your requirements.

SAP Sales Cloud DVP Add-On:

  • Dynamic Visit Planning Add-On | Outstation Planning - Dynamic visit planning now allows to curate plans with stays away from office/home to manage territories.
  • Dynamic Visit Planning Add-On | Plan my day on filtered list - Plan my day would only focus on accounts with highest score, Now it respects the filters as well.

SAP Service Cloud:

  • Support in C4C Ticket for FSM Data Retention in C4C-FSM Integration - When a service call object is marked for soft delete or hard delete by the data retention policy in FSM by an Administrator, the corresponding replicated ticket still remains in C4C. Any update on C4C ticket will not trigger an outbound sync to FSM anymore.
  • Enabled Read/Write access (PSM release) for Maintenance Plan field MaintenancePlanNextRunMDROID

SAP Agent Desktop Add-On:

  • Agent Desktop Add-On | New Ticket Form on Incoming Call Acceptance - The 'New Ticket Create' form would display alongside the Agent Desktop session when the agent accepts an incoming phone call.

Thanks,

SAP Sales Cloud & SAP Service Cloud Product Management Team

entry.title: Need to turn off specific Ariba Supplier Notifications going out in TEST
entry.content:
For background I work on SAP ECC, so I'm a novice with Ariba.

We've been live with Ariba for a while now, but are still on-boarding suppliers a handful at a time. When we work with suppliers to on-board them, we first on-board them in the TEST Ariba environment and once everything passes, then they are on-boarded to Production Ariba. While onboarding in TEST they want the notifications to be sent to the Supplier, etc. But after they are on-boarded to Production Ariba, they want to turn off the notifications in TEST and only send the supplier notifications in Production. But they still want to send notifications out of TEST for other suppliers that are still testing the onboard process.

So basically, turn off notifications in TEST for most suppliers and only have the notifications in TEST for the small set of suppliers currently being onboarded in TEST.

We had a situation where the MPR jobs were run wide open in our QA SAP system and it pushed a bunch of updates to TEST Ariba that also sent notifications to a bunch of suppliers in TEST. The suppliers thought the notifications were production notifications (even though the notifications said TEST) so they logged a bunch of issues with us thinking they were getting duplicate data in production. Our business is asking that we NEVER run MRP in QA wide open, but there really isn't a good way to prevent this and there are times where we may need to run it wide open in our QA environment for valid testing reasons. I don't think this is a valid long-term solution.

Is there a way for us to control the notifications we send out of TEST Ariba? Or does each supplier have to go in and turn off the TEST notifications themselves?

Or is there a better way to handle this that I'm not currently thinking of?

Thanks

Xavier

entry.title: Re: Does PRIMARY KEY clustered check stored somewhere
entry.content:
You should start a new thread for the SQL_NTS question. I have tested with the query from Wednesday (where you identified the issue as resolved - I note this only because of the multiple queries in thread).

Here are the bindings:

SQLBindCol(stmt, 1, SQL_C_WCHAR, 
    tableName, sizeof(tableName), &cbTable);
SQLBindCol(stmt, 2, SQL_C_ULONG, 
    &clusterIdx, sizeof(clusterIdx), &cbclustered);
SQLBindCol(stmt, 3, SQL_C_WCHAR, 
    idxName, sizeof(idxName), &cbIdxName);
SQLBindCol(stmt, 4, SQL_C_WCHAR, 
    idxType, sizeof(idxType), &cbIdxType);

  and the code that displays the results

printf( "%ls,%ls,%ls,ls%\n", 
    (wchar_t *)tableName,
    (cbclustered==SQL_NULL_DATA ? L"(NULL)":L"Clustered"),
    (wchar_t *)idxName,
    (wchar_t *)idxType );

And the actual output (note that I am testing a table name T with a primary key name T:

[C:\2026\forum\Clustered]odbcselect.exe
t,(NULL),t,ls

The second column reports (NULL) because the indicator for the clustered index column is returned as SQL_NULL_DATA.

entry.title: [Utilities] SAP Learning Curriculum for Utilities
entry.content:
The Utilities industry is evolving rapidly - and so is the way we learn in SAP for Utilities.

To better support our learners, we have introduced new and updated Utilities training courses, while older Utilities courses have been retired. This blog helps you understand what this change means for you and how to continue your learning journey with clarity and confidence.

Below is an overview of the key new Utilities courses, with links to both learning.sap.com (learning journeys & overviews) and training.sap.com (official SAP-delivered ILT / virtual classes):
 

1. S4IUT – Executing Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • End‑to‑end introduction to SAP S/4HANA Utilities business processes

    • Provides a comprehensive overview of core Utilities business processes in SAP S/4HANA
    • Covers Meter‑to‑Cash, Customer Engagement, Contract and Account Management, FI‑CA, and Device Management at process level
    • Focuses on how Utilities processes work end‑to‑end rather than on detailed configuration
    • Designed for:
      • Utilities consultants and business analysts
      • Project team members
      • Learners new to SAP S/4HANA Utilities

    Typically positioned as the primary entry point into SAP S/4HANA Utilities learning.

  • LSC Link: Discovering Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/s4iut

2. IUT21 – Configuring Master Data and Basic Functions in SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • Foundation course for Utilities configuration and core setup

    • Focuses on Utilities master data objects and basic configuration in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
    • Covers:
      • Business partners and contracts
      • Premises and connection objects
      • Basic process integration
    • Suitable for:
      • Implementation consultants
      • Configuration‑focused project team members
      • Learners moving from functional overview to system setup

    Typically positioned as the first configuration course following an end‑to‑end process overview.

  • LSC Link: Configuring Master Data and Basic Functions in SAP S/4HANA Utilit
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/iut21

3. IUT22 – Configuring Device Management in SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • In‑depth configuration of Utilities Device Management

    • Deep dive into Device Management in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
    • Covers:
      • Technical objects and installations
      • Meter reading, inspections, and technical processes
      • Integration of Device Management with commercial processes
    • Designed for:
      • Utilities technical consultants
      • Device Management specialists
      • Project roles focusing on metering and technical assets

    Typically positioned as an advanced follow‑up after core Utilities configuration.

  • LSC Link: Configuring Device Management in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/iut22

4. IUT23 – Configuring Billing and Invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • Advanced course for Utilities billing and invoicing processes

    • Focuses on Utilities‑specific billing and invoicing in SAP S/4HANA
    • Covers:
      • Tariff structures and billing schemas
      • Consumption‑based billing
      • Integration between billing, invoicing, and FI components
    • Intended for:
      • Billing and invoicing consultants
      • Project team members responsible for revenue processes
      • Advanced Utilities configuration roles

    Typically positioned as an advanced configuration course, building on master data and device management knowledge.

  • LSC Link: Configuring Billing and Invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/iut23 

S4IUD – Transitioning to SAP S/4HANA for Utilities

  • Understanding the transition from SAP ECC IS‑U to SAP S/4HANA Utilities

    • Designed for Utilities customers and partners planning or executing a system transition
    • Covers:
      • SAP S/4HANA Utilities architecture
      • Transition and conversion scenarios
      • Impact on business processes and system landscape
      • Introduction to Fiori and SAP BTP context for Utilities
    • Suitable for:
      • Architects and senior consultants
      • Project leads
      • Customers preparing for S/4HANA Utilities adoption

    Typically positioned as a strategic orientation course for Utilities transition projects.

  • LSC link: ​​Transitioning to SAP S/4HANA for Utilities​
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/S4IUD
 

German market‑specific learning offering

IUT51 – SAP Market Communication for Utilities in the German Energy Market

  • Market‑specific introduction to SAP market communication processes for German utilities

    • Provides a structured introduction to SAP Market Communication (MaCo) in the deregulated German energy market, focusing on the regulatory and process context specific to Germany
    • Explains the evolution of market communication in SAP Utilities, including the role of energy market deregulation and its impact on Utilities business processes
    • Introduces the architecture and core components used by SAP to support market communication scenarios, including:
      • Application Process Engine (APE)
      • MaCo‑Cloud
    • Describes how regulatory changes have influenced SAP IS‑U and IDEX‑based processes, and how SAP is shifting these scenarios toward cloud‑based automation
    • Provides insight into key process flows and master data concepts used for market communication in Germany, based on SAP’s Utilities reference data model
    • Includes exposure to SAP tooling and content through:
      • SAP Fiori apps
      • Demonstrations of MSB billing scenarios
      • Exploration of SAP’s reference data structures for German Utilities market communication

    Designed for:

    • Architects
    • Consultants
    • Business users

    working with market communication processes in the German Utilities industry.

    This course is positioned as a specialized, market‑specific learning offering and builds on an existing understanding of SAP S/4HANA Utilities end‑to‑end business processes.

  • LSC link: Explore SAP Market Communication for Utilities in the German Energy Market

IUT580 – SAP Market Communication for Utilities, focus on Application Process Engine (APE)

  • Technical overview of SAP Market Communication for Utilities with focus on APE

    • Provides a comprehensive technical overview of SAP Market Communication for Utilities with a focus on the Application Process Engine (APE)
    • Explains the system architecture and technical fundamentals of SAP Market Communication solutions
    • Covers user interfaces, applications, and integration aspects used in market communication scenarios
    • Introduces APE architecture and functionality, including configuration, processes, checks, exceptions, and deadlines
    • Explains how to adapt standard market communication processes or create custom processes using APE, business objects, and Business Add‑Ins (BAdIs)

    Designed for:

    • Enterprise and solution architects
    • Senior technical and application consultants
  • Typically positioned as a technical deep‑dive course for designing, extending, and controlling SAP Market Communication processes using APE.
  • ILT / Virtual training: https://training.sap.com/course/iut580

What’s changing in Utilities learning?

Over the past years, SAP Utilities solutions, architectures, and business processes have significantly evolved. To reflect this:

  •  New Utilities courses and learning journeys are now available
  •  Older Utilities-specific courses have been retired and are no longer maintained
  •  The learning focus has shifted to modern SAP solutions, industry processes, and current best practices

This means that if you previously started learning Utilities with older courses, now is the right time to review and update your learning path.

Why new courses?

The new Utilities learning content is designed to:

  • Align with current SAP solution portfolios
  • Focus on end-to-end industry processes
  • Support learners in different roles (consultants, architects, business professionals)
  • Serve as a foundation for project readiness and continuous learning

Instead of isolated topics, the new courses help you understand:

  • How SAP addresses Utilities industry challenges today
  • Which topics are essential depending on your role
  • Where to continue learning next

What should you do if you learned with the old courses?

If you used older Utilities trainings in the past:

  • Consider them good historical context, but not a current learning baseline
  • Use the new courses to refresh and modernize your knowledge
  • Combine new Utilities learning with related SAP platform topics (for example analytics, integration, or cloud fundamentals)

This is a natural evolution and many learners are currently making the same transition.

You don’t have to do this alone

To support this transition, the Industries Learning Group was created as a dedicated place to talk about learning - not technical implementation.

In this group, you can:

  • Exchange with other Utilities learners
  • Ask questions like:
    • Which Utilities course should I start with now?
    • How do the new courses fit together?
    • What should I learn next after completing a course?
  • Share feedback on learning journeys and enablement content

The goal is simple: help each other navigate SAP learning for industries more easily.

How Utilities content is structured in the group

All posts related to Utilities are clearly marked:

  • Utilities in the title (for example: [Utilities] Learning Path Overview)
  • Utilities tags, so you can filter and follow relevant content

This makes it easy to stay focused on what matters to you.

Join the Industries Learning Group

If you are:

  • Starting your SAP Utilities learning journey
  • Transitioning from retired courses to the new ones
  • Looking for orientation before going deeper into technical topics
 👉 Join the Industries Learning Group
 👉 Follow Utilities‑related posts
 👉 Exchange with other learners

This group is your learning starting point.
For deep technical or implementation discussions, we will gladly guide you to the right expert communities.

Let’s learn SAP for Utilities - together.

Greg

entry.title: Purchase Card Integration with SAP Concur
entry.content:
Introduction: SAP Concur seamlessly integrates purchasing cards (P‑cards) with expense management by automatically importing and categorizing transactions. This automation simplifies expense review, reconciliation, and approval while enhancing visibility into spending trends for better data analysis and vendor negotiations. The system enforces policy rules, manages workflows, and aligns invoices with pre-approved purchases to ensure accuracy and compliance.

Follow the below steps to integrate Purchase Card with SAP Concur:

Step-1: Create the Purchase Card Policy

Navigate to Administration > Expense > Policies. Select any existing policy and click on “Copy Policy” to create new policy

Fill in all the required details to configure the policy. Review all the information entered and click on “Save” to create the Purchase card Policy.

Step 2: Configure Purchase Card Feed Import Definition

  • To integrate purchase card with SAP Concur, a Partner case must be raised with Concur for the feed setup.
  • Once the purchase card integration setup is completed, all purchase cards will be mapped automatically to the employees.

Step 3: Review Purchase Cards mapping to the Card Program

Ensure each purchase card account is correctly linked to its corresponding card program. Proper association enables accurate expense tracking, smoother reconciliation, and more reliable reporting.

Only users with the Company Card Administrator role have permission to assign individual purchasing card accounts to a program.

  1. Navigate to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card > Manage Accounts.
  2. Search for purchase card accounts by using the Search in options such as Name on Card, Employee Name, or Employee ID as same as corporate cards

  1. Select the desired row and choose Modify to open the Modify Card Account dialog box.

  1. Select the appropriate Card Program Name (required).
  2. Click Save.

Step 4: Review Merchant Codes to Expense Types Mappings

Ensure all merchant category codes (MCCs) are correctly mapped to expense types per Purchase Card policy for accurate classification and compliance.

To review and update merchant code mappings:

  1. Go to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card > Merchant Code Mapping.

  1. Verify all MCCs are present and correctly labelled, mapped to the right expense types.
  2. For each expense type, check its status:
  • If mapped, select it, click Modify to verify or add MCCs via Map New.
  • If Not Mapped, select it, click Modify > Map New, map respective merchant code.
  1. To configure MCC settings, select an expense type, click Modify, choose the MCC, and click Modify again.
  2. Review Expense Type & merchant code mappings and click “Next” to continue.

  1. Review the mappings, make any necessary adjustments, and then click Done.

Step 5: Validate Purchase Card Settings

Review the purchase card settings:

  1. Navigate to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card.
  2. Select the Settings tab.

  1. Modify the settings accordingly.
  2. Click on “Save”

Benefits of Using Purchase Card Integration with SAP Concur

  1. Automatic Data Feed: P‑Card transactions are automatically imported from the bank into Concur, eliminating manual expense entry and reducing user errors.
  2. Real-Time Financial Insight: Finance teams get real‑time insight into purchasing activity across departments, vendors, and cost centers through dashboards and reports.
  3. Stronger Policy Compliance: Concur enforces expense rules (MCC restrictions, limits, receipt requirements) at transaction level, reducing non‑compliant spending.
  4. Reduced Invoice Processing Costs: P‑Cards minimize low‑value invoices and AP workload by shifting spend directly to card transactions.

General FAQs:

Q1. What is a Purchase Card (P-Card) in SAP Concur?

A:  A company-issued payment card for purchases, with transactions automatically imported into SAP Concur for employee review and processing.

Q2. Can employees delete P-Card transactions?

A:  No, transactions cannot be deleted but can be reconciled, flagged, commented on, or returned based on permissions.

Q3. What if an expense report is submitted without all P-Card transactions assigned?

A:  Concur may block submission or alert users; unassigned transactions remain in Available Expenses until reconciled.

Q4. How does SAP Concur ensure P-Card compliance?

A: By enforcing policies through rules, audits, receipt requirements, and approval workflows before reimbursement or reconciliation.

entry.title: Reconhecimento da receita na entrega de mercadoria
entry.content:
configurando o fluxo de faturamento antecipado estou feito (Entrega Futura) e o sistema está ignorando o diferencial da receita, registrando o crédito na conta de resultado logo na emissão da NF da Futura.

Cenário Atual (O Problema)

  • NF de Saída (Futura): O sistema está creditando diretamente a conta de Receita ( 3.01.01.01.01), quando deveria creditar a conta Redutora ( 1.01.03.01.03).

  • Entrega: Como a receita já foi confiável na nota, a entrega realiza apenas a baixa de estoque (Estoque vs CPV) e impostos, deixando de fazer o "swap" contábil (Redutora vs Receita).

O Objetivo (Fluxo Correto)

O objetivo é que a contabilização siga o princípio da competência, onde a receita só "nasce" com a saída física:

  1. Na NF de Saída (Futura):

    • Débito: PN (Cliente)

    • Crédito: 1.01.03.01.03 ((-) Clientes Nacionais Venda Futura) — Conta Redutora/Passivo.

  2. Na Entrega:

    • Débito: 1.01.03.01.03 (Limpa a conta redutora).

    • Crédito: 3.01.01.01.01 (Reconhece a Receita de Venda).

    • Mais os lançamentos de CPV e Impostos.

 

Ambiente:

  • Versão: SAP Business One versão 10.0 para SAP HANA (10.00.200) FP 2208.

"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\JobServerChild.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\sia.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\WIReportServer.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\shared.tp.aurora.installmedia.jre-4.0-core-64\14.3.4.5099\jre\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x86\sapjvm\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x86\sapjvm\jre\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\sapjvm\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\sapjvm\jre\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\BIN64\jre180\bin\java.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\BIN64\dbsrv17.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\tomcat\bin\tomcat9.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\setup.engine\plat64\ActionAgentProc.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\setup.engine\actionagentproc.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\setup.exe"
"D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\setup.engine\setupengine.exe"

- I set the following registry key to 1 for the management of files with very long paths:
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\LongPathsEnabled
- I renamed the folder:
D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\ in InstallCache.bak 
to have the upgrade tool recreate it.
- I have disabled, on the D drive, the Windows Search Indexing.
- I removed the "read only" attribute from the D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData folder.
- I started the Process Monitor tool.

In the dialog phase of the upgrade, immediately after entering the login credentials, I stopped the SIA, Tomcat and SQL Anywhere.

Again, after a few minutes, the "ErrorsAndWarnings.log" file was populated with the usual errors.
In the log file, I can see the following line:

Error: Could not move temp dest folder to dest folder.Error:   Temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\webi.cdp.dsl_plugin-4.0-da-nuError:   Dest folder:    D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\webi.cdp.dsl_plugin-4.0-da-nu\14.3.5.5657\

And I can find a trace, in the Process Monitor logs with “ACCESS DENIED” result, about the same folder.

The problem is that I don't understand how to identify the cause of this ACCESS DENIED in the proc.mon. event. How can I do this? (Unfortunately this forum doesn't allow me to upload either CSV or PML files 😞 to show you).

Please help me.
Kind regards.
Andrea

entry.title: SAP ALM Experience Day - Masterclass Edition - Vancouver, Canada!
entry.content:
Join us at the SAP ALM Experience Day – Masterclass Edition and gain a comprehensive, hands-on perspective on managing on‑premise, cloud, and hybrid landscapes with confidence.

What to expect:

  • Deep dive into SAP Cloud ALM strategy and clean core principles
  • Best practices for RISE with SAP and integrated toolchains
  • Real-world insights from SAP ALM experts
  • Hands-on enablement, peer networking, and optional certification opportunities

Location : In‑person | No cost | SAP Office in Vancouver
This immersive experience is designed to help you drive faster implementations, smarter operations, and greater value from your SAP landscape.

Register now: https://events.sap.com/sap-alm-events-na/en_us/home.html

entry.title: FIORI for DP90 (contract billing) in S/4 HANA service
entry.content:
Will FIORI like DP90 selection screen available in S/4 PERSON_NAME service to do the billing based on contract sales order?

If not then will there be any SAP FIORI app available in S/4 PERSON_NAME service that can provide an option to input contract sales order number on selection screen to  fetch the cost of all associated service order(linked to contract sales order)?

  


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: FIORI for DP90 (contract billing) in S/4 HANA service
entry.content: The availability of a FIORI-like DP90 selection screen in S/4 HANA service for contract billing is uncertain.

entry.title: New Course: Getting Started with Joule for Business Users
entry.content:
We are excited to launch a new course designed for business users who want to get the most out of SAP Joule. This course provides practical guidance on using natural language to retrieve information, complete tasks, and make better business decisions. Whether you are new to SAP or looking to deepen your expertise, this course will help you improve your daily workflows and productivity.

 

Summary:

  • Use Joule to interact with SAP systems using simple, natural language commands.
  • Retrieve information and complete common business tasks more efficiently.
  • Apply effective prompting techniques for accurate and relevant results.
  • Manage ongoing conversations within different SAP business functions.
  • Leverage Joule’s insights to support better, data-driven decision-making.

Start Learning: Getting Started with Joule for Business Users
